_meta.authoringNotes: Epidemiology from Rushworth et al. Eur J Endocrinol 2012 (PMID 22577124) and Barthel et al. Eur J Endocrinol 2013. Clinical management per Endocrine Society 2016 guidelines on adrenal insufficiency. Mortality data from Smans et al. Eur J Endocrinol 2016 (PMID 26637427).

h1Title: Addisonian Crisis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: An addisonian crisis — also called acute adrenal crisis — is a life-threatening collapse of cortisol and aldosterone production that causes severe hypotension, vomiting, and altered consciousness. Population studies estimate that patients with known adrenal insufficiency experience roughly 5-8 adrenal crises per 100 patient-years, with a case-fatality rate of approximately 0.5 per 100 patient-years even in treated cohorts. Most crises are triggered by intercurrent illness, surgery, or accidental omission of glucocorticoid replacement. Immediate intramuscular or intravenous hydrocortisone (100 mg bolus) is the definitive first-aid measure and must precede confirmatory testing when crisis is clinically suspected. With prompt treatment, recovery within 24-48 hours is the rule; delayed treatment is fatal.

keyStats.prevalence: 5-8 adrenal crises per 100 patient-years among people with established adrenal insufficiency (Rushworth et al. 2012)
keyStats.demographics: Primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease) most common in women aged 30-50; secondary adrenal insufficiency from glucocorticoid withdrawal affects all ages
keyStats.avgAge: First crisis typically occurs in third to fifth decade; crisis risk remains lifelong once adrenal insufficiency is established
keyStats.globalCases: Addison disease affects roughly 100-140 per million people in Western populations; adrenal insufficiency overall (all causes) affects up to 500 per million

definition: Addisonian crisis (ICD-10: E27.2), also called acute adrenal insufficiency or adrenal crisis, is a medical emergency arising from absolute or relative deficiency of both cortisol and, in primary adrenal insufficiency, aldosterone. The hallmark is hemodynamic collapse resistant to fluid resuscitation without concurrent glucocorticoid replacement. Primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease, autoimmune destruction of the adrenal cortex) accounts for roughly 80% of cases in non-iatrogenic populations; secondary adrenal insufficiency from pituitary failure or, most commonly, abrupt withdrawal of exogenous glucocorticoid therapy accounts for the remainder. In secondary disease aldosterone secretion is usually preserved, making hypotension less prominent but not absent. Crisis is defined physiologically by a cortisol level below 18 µg/dL (500 nmol/L) in the context of physiological stress, although treatment must not await biochemical confirmation. The Endocrine Society and the European Society of Endocrinology classify crisis severity by hemodynamic instability, nausea and vomiting that prevent oral replacement, and need for intravenous therapy. Endocrinology and emergency medicine jointly manage the acute presentation; long-term management is led by endocrinology.

typesClassification.type: Crisis in primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease)
typesClassification.description: Both cortisol and aldosterone are deficient because the adrenal cortex is destroyed — most commonly by autoimmune adrenalitis, tuberculosis, or bilateral hemorrhage. Severe hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and hypotension from aldosterone deficiency are prominent. Skin hyperpigmentation is a clue to chronically elevated ACTH.

ACTH deficiency from pituitary or hypothalamic disease leaves the adrenal cortex atrophied. Cortisol is deficient but mineralocorticoid secretion is largely intact, so severe hyperkalemia is uncommon. Hypoglycemia is more prominent than in primary disease. Most cases follow pituitary surgery, head trauma, or long-term glucocorticoid therapy withdrawal.

typesClassification.type: Crisis from bilateral adrenal hemorrhage (Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome)
typesClassification.description: Acute bilateral adrenal hemorrhage during septicemia — classically meningococcal — destroys functioning adrenal tissue within hours. Presents as fulminant septic shock with petechial or purpuric rash, DIC, and abrupt cortisol collapse. CT abdomen shows bilateral adrenal masses.

Approximately 50% of adrenal crises occur in patients with previously unrecognized adrenal insufficiency; an intercurrent illness unmasks latent insufficiency. Presenting illness may be misattributed to gastroenteritis, sepsis, or an abdominal emergency before the diagnosis is made.

primarySymptoms: Profound hypotension with systolic blood pressure below 100 mmHg that does not respond to a 1-2 L fluid challenge, reflecting both volume depletion and lack of cortisol-mediated vascular tone.
Severe nausea, repeated vomiting, and abdominal pain — sometimes mimicking an acute abdomen — that prevent oral glucocorticoid intake and accelerate dehydration.
Intense fatigue, profound weakness, and inability to stand or walk without support, caused by cortisol and aldosterone deficiency affecting muscle and cardiovascular function.
Confusion, disorientation, or reduced level of consciousness from cerebral hypoperfusion, hypoglycemia, and hyponatremia acting together.
Hypoglycemia with blood glucose below 3.5 mmol/L (63 mg/dL), particularly in secondary adrenal insufficiency and in children, from loss of cortisol-driven gluconeogenesis.
Severe hyponatremia (serum sodium below 130 mmol/L) in primary adrenal insufficiency from aldosterone deficiency causing urinary sodium wasting and free-water retention.
Hyperkalemia (serum potassium above 5.5 mmol/L) in primary adrenal insufficiency, sometimes severe enough to cause cardiac arrhythmias.
High fever or, paradoxically, hypothermia; fever may reflect the precipitating infection or the crisis itself, making diagnosis in septic patients particularly challenging.
Diffuse hyperpigmentation of skin creases, buccal mucosa, scars, and nipples in patients with chronic primary adrenal insufficiency — a diagnostic clue when present.
Rapid heart rate (tachycardia above 100 bpm) with weak peripheral pulses as the cardiovascular system attempts to compensate for reduced preload and afterload.
earlyWarningSigns: Increasing fatigue, dizziness on standing (orthostatic hypotension), and nausea developing over hours to a day before frank crisis in a patient with known adrenal insufficiency under physical or emotional stress.
Failure to respond to usual stress-dosing of hydrocortisone within 2-4 hours during an intercurrent illness, suggesting the oral dose is insufficient or is not being absorbed.

Serum sodium falling below 135 mmol/L on a routine blood test in a patient with Addison disease — a warning sign that aldosterone replacement needs adjustment or that a precipitating illness is present.
Unexplained low-grade hypoglycemia (blood glucose 3.5-4.0 mmol/L) in a known glucocorticoid-dependent patient, particularly in the morning before the first dose.
Fever above 38°C with vomiting in a patient on glucocorticoid therapy who cannot keep tablets down — this combination mandates emergency injection of hydrocortisone.
emergencySigns: Systolic blood pressure below 90 mmHg unresponsive to 1 L of normal saline — inject hydrocortisone 100 mg IM or IV immediately and call emergency services.
Loss of consciousness, seizure, or severe confusion in a known or suspected adrenal-insufficient patient, particularly with hypoglycemia on bedside glucose testing.

Profuse petechiae or purpura with high fever and hemodynamic collapse suggesting Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome — this is immediately life-threatening and requires hydrocortisone plus empirical antibiotics.
Uncontrollable vomiting in a patient with adrenal insufficiency who cannot absorb oral hydrocortisone — the parenteral route must be used without delay.
New-onset severe abdominal pain, guarding, and hemodynamic instability after anticoagulation therapy or in a patient with antiphospholipid syndrome — consider bilateral adrenal hemorrhage.

causes.cause: Physiological stress without adequate glucocorticoid dose increase
causes.description: Infection (most commonly gastroenteritis or respiratory illness), surgery, trauma, labor, or acute psychological stress approximately double to quadruple cortisol requirements. Patients on fixed-dose replacement who do not triple their hydrocortisone during illness develop relative insufficiency within hours. This is the precipitating factor in 30-50% of crises in published series.

causes.cause: Failure to inject emergency hydrocortisone when vomiting prevents oral intake
causes.description: Once vomiting occurs, oral hydrocortisone is not absorbed. Patients who lack an emergency injection kit or do not know when to use it progress rapidly from prodromal nausea to full hemodynamic collapse within 2-6 hours.

causes.cause: Abrupt or rapid withdrawal of chronic glucocorticoid therapy
causes.description: Patients who have received pharmacological doses of corticosteroids for more than 3 weeks develop hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis suppression. Stopping treatment abruptly — or reducing too quickly — leaves the adrenal cortex unable to mount a cortisol response to stress for months.

Acute hemorrhage during anticoagulation therapy, antiphospholipid syndrome, or septicemia (classically Neisseria meningitidis causing Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome) destroys adrenal tissue over hours. Adrenal vein thrombosis and adrenal metastases can also cause progressive cortisol deficiency that tips into crisis.

causes.cause: Pituitary apoplexy or acute pituitary failure
causes.description: Hemorrhage or infarction of a pituitary adenoma causes sudden ACTH deficiency, leaving the adrenal cortex producing no cortisol within 12-24 hours. Presents with severe headache, visual field defects, and rapid hemodynamic collapse requiring both glucocorticoid and general supportive care.

causes.cause: Newly diagnosed adrenal insufficiency unmasked by illness
causes.description: Up to 50% of first crises occur in patients who did not know they had adrenal insufficiency. An intercurrent illness overwhelms borderline adrenal reserve, and crisis is the presenting event for autoimmune Addison disease, bilateral adrenal tuberculosis, or long-term suppression from intermittent steroid use.

riskFactors.description: Lifetime risk of crisis is near-universal without optimal education and emergency supplies. Population studies report 6-8 crises per 100 patient-years; cumulative lifetime risk approaches 50% without patient training.

riskFactors.factor: Secondary adrenal insufficiency from glucocorticoid therapy
riskFactors.description: Patients taking oral, inhaled, topical, or injected steroids equivalent to ≥7.5 mg/day prednisolone for ≥3 weeks can develop HPA-axis suppression. The risk rises with dose, duration, and nighttime dosing.

riskFactors.factor: Pituitary disease or prior pituitary surgery
riskFactors.description: ACTH deficiency from pituitary adenoma, craniopharyngioma, hypophysitis, or pituitary surgery removes the drive to cortisol production; crisis can occur without autoimmune features or electrolyte abnormalities of primary disease.

riskFactors.factor: Lack of sick-day rule education and emergency injection kit
riskFactors.description: Studies show that 40-70% of adrenal crisis patients had no injectable hydrocortisone at home. Education in sick-day rules and provision of an emergency kit reduces crisis rate by approximately 50% in trained cohorts.

Vomiting prevents oral glucocorticoid absorption at exactly the moment when requirements are highest. Gastroenteritis is the most common precipitant in published crisis registries, accounting for 20-30% of episodes.

Type 1 (AIRE gene mutation) and type 2 autoimmune polyglandular syndromes associate Addison disease with hypothyroidism, type 1 diabetes, hypoparathyroidism, or mucocutaneous candidiasis, increasing the frequency of stressors that can trigger crisis.

Patients on warfarin, heparin, or direct oral anticoagulants are at risk of spontaneous bilateral adrenal hemorrhage, which can present as sudden adrenal crisis without prior adrenal insufficiency. Antiphospholipid syndrome carries an independent risk.

affectedDemographics: Primary adrenal insufficiency affects women two to three times more often than men, peak incidence in the third to fifth decade, predominantly in populations of European ancestry where autoimmune Addison disease is most common.

Secondary adrenal insufficiency from glucocorticoid withdrawal is the most common cause of adrenal insufficiency worldwide, affecting both sexes equally and all age groups; estimated prevalence 150-280 per 100,000 in populations with high glucocorticoid prescription rates.
Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome is most common in children and adolescents during the peak incidence years for meningococcal disease (under 5 and 15-19 years).

Elderly patients on long-term glucocorticoids for rheumatologic, respiratory, or inflammatory bowel disease represent the largest growing group at risk; many are unaware their therapy carries adrenal-crisis risk.
diagnosisOverview: Adrenal crisis is diagnosed clinically — a patient with known adrenal insufficiency presenting with hypotension, vomiting, and inability to self-medicate requires immediate hydrocortisone without waiting for laboratory confirmation. In a patient with unknown adrenal status, the clinical triad of unexplained hypotension, hyponatremia, and hyperkalemia should trigger high suspicion. When time permits, blood is drawn for a random cortisol, ACTH, electrolytes, blood glucose, and full blood count before hydrocortisone administration; however, treatment must never be delayed for test results. A random cortisol below 18 µg/dL (500 nmol/L) in the context of severe physiological stress is consistent with adrenal insufficiency, but the interpretation depends on assay and clinical context. After stabilization, a Short Synacthen (ACTH stimulation) test — 250 µg cosyntropin IV with cortisol measured at 0 and 30 minutes — is the standard confirmatory test: a peak cortisol below 18-20 µg/dL (500-550 nmol/L) confirms primary or secondary adrenal insufficiency. ACTH measurement (elevated in primary, low or normal in secondary) distinguishes the two. Imaging of the adrenal glands by CT or MRI is performed electively to identify hemorrhage, metastasis, tuberculosis, or other structural causes. Pituitary MRI is indicated when secondary adrenal insufficiency is confirmed.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Initial screen; a level below 18 µg/dL (500 nmol/L) in a critically ill patient is consistent with adrenal insufficiency
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw, ideally collected before giving hydrocortisone. Results return within 1-2 hours. Levels are interpreted alongside clinical status, not in isolation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes primary (high ACTH) from secondary (low or inappropriately normal ACTH) adrenal insufficiency
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood collected in a cold EDTA tube and transported immediately on ice; laboratory turnaround 2-4 hours. Must be drawn before glucocorticoid administration to avoid suppression.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard confirmatory test; peak cortisol below 18-20 µg/dL (500-550 nmol/L) confirms adrenal insufficiency
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 250 µg of cosyntropin is injected IV or IM; cortisol is drawn at 0, 30, and sometimes 60 minutes. Performed electively after acute stabilization, not during active crisis.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum electrolytes, glucose, and renal function
diagnosticTests.purpose: Reveals hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, hypoglycemia, and volume-depletion nephropathy that characterize crisis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Routine blood draw. Results available within 30-60 minutes in most emergency departments. Guides fluid and electrolyte replacement targets.

diagnosticTests.test: CT abdomen and pelvis without contrast
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies bilateral adrenal hemorrhage, adrenal masses, metastases, or calcification from prior tuberculosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 15-minute scan lying still. No contrast needed for hemorrhage detection; adrenals appear hyperdense on non-contrast CT within days of bleeding.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms autoimmune Addison disease in primary adrenal insufficiency; positive in about 90% of autoimmune cases
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A routine blood draw sent to a reference laboratory. Results in 3-7 days. A negative result in primary adrenal insufficiency prompts search for non-autoimmune causes.

treatmentOverview: Immediate treatment of adrenal crisis follows a three-step protocol endorsed by the Endocrine Society (2016) and the European Society of Endocrinology: give hydrocortisone, give intravenous fluids, and manage the precipitant. Hydrocortisone 100 mg is injected IV bolus or IM (deltoid or thigh) immediately, followed by 200 mg/24 h as a continuous infusion or 50 mg IM every 6 hours. At this dose, hydrocortisone also has sufficient mineralocorticoid activity to correct sodium and potassium; fludrocortisone is not needed acutely. Intravenous 0.9% saline at 1 L/hour for the first 1-2 hours corrects volume depletion and dilutional hyponatremia, with 5% dextrose added when blood glucose is below 3.5 mmol/L. The precipitating cause — usually infection — is treated concurrently; empirical antibiotics are started when infection is suspected. Once nausea resolves and the patient can take oral fluids, hydrocortisone is stepped down to three-times-daily oral dosing (e.g., 20-10-10 mg) and eventually to the patient's pre-crisis maintenance dose over 2-3 days. Mineralocorticoid replacement with fludrocortisone 0.1 mg/day is resumed in primary adrenal insufficiency as oral intake resumes. Prevention of future crises requires written sick-day rules, a home emergency injection kit (hydrocortisone 100 mg powder for reconstitution), a medical alert identification, and structured patient education — interventions shown to reduce crisis recurrence by up to 50%. The Endocrine Society recommends annual re-education reviews for all patients with adrenal insufficiency.

medicalTreatments.name: Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV/IM bolus (immediate)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line emergency treatment. Given immediately on clinical suspicion without waiting for cortisol results. Corrects glucocorticoid deficiency and, at supraphysiological doses, provides sufficient mineralocorticoid effect to stabilize hemodynamics.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reversal of hemodynamic collapse in 30-60 minutes in the majority of patients; mortality in treated patients is approximately 0.5 per 100 patient-years versus near-universal fatality if untreated

medicalTreatments.name: Hydrocortisone 200 mg/24 h continuous IV infusion (or 50 mg IM q6h)
medicalTreatments.description: Maintenance after initial bolus, continued until oral intake is tolerated. Tapering begins when patient is clinically stable — usually at 24-48 hours.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Sustained hemodynamic stability achieved in 80-90% of patients within 12-24 hours of starting infusion

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous 0.9% sodium chloride (1-2 L in first 2 hours)
medicalTreatments.description: Corrects hypovolemia, dilutes hyperkalemia, and partly corrects hyponatremia. Subsequent rate guided by blood pressure, urine output, and electrolytes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Combined with hydrocortisone, normalizes blood pressure in 85-90% of patients within 4-6 hours

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous dextrose (5% or 10%) when blood glucose is below 3.5 mmol/L
medicalTreatments.description: Prevents and treats hypoglycemia, which is particularly prominent in secondary adrenal insufficiency and children. Added to normal saline or given as a bolus of 50 mL 50% dextrose.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Raises blood glucose within 15-30 minutes; maintained with ongoing dextrose-containing fluids

medicalTreatments.name: Fludrocortisone 0.1 mg oral daily (primary adrenal insufficiency)
medicalTreatments.description: Mineralocorticoid replacement reinstated once oral intake is tolerated to maintain sodium balance and prevent recurrent volume depletion. Not required during IV hydrocortisone phase.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Normalizes serum potassium and sodium within 3-7 days; essential for long-term outpatient blood pressure stability in primary adrenal insufficiency

medicalTreatments.name: Empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics (when infection suspected)
medicalTreatments.description: Infection is the most common precipitant. When a bacterial source is suspected (fever, elevated WBC, localizing signs), empirical treatment is started simultaneously with hydrocortisone — the two must not be delayed waiting for the other.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 30-day mortality from adrenal crisis halved when antibiotic treatment of the precipitating infection begins within 1 hour of presentation

alternativeTreatments.name: Patient-administered emergency hydrocortisone injection training
alternativeTreatments.description: A structured program teaching patients or caregivers to self-inject hydrocortisone 100 mg IM reduces crisis-related hospitalizations by approximately 40-50% in controlled observational studies. This is endorsed by the Endocrine Society as standard of care, not a complementary intervention.

whySeeSpecialist: An endocrinologist should be involved in every diagnosis of adrenal insufficiency to determine cause, optimize long-term replacement dosing, prescribe emergency kits, and provide structured sick-day rule education. Patients whose crisis is the first presentation of adrenal insufficiency need investigation of the underlying etiology — autoimmune, pituitary, or structural.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an endocrinologist with experience managing adrenal insufficiency who can provide written sick-day rules, prescribe an emergency hydrocortisone injection kit, and offer annual review. For crisis in the context of pituitary disease, a pituitary center offering endocrinology and neurosurgery in one pathway is preferred.
hospitalCriteria: Access to emergency IV hydrocortisone and IV fluid administration around the clock
Ability to measure serum cortisol, ACTH, and electrolytes urgently
Endocrinology inpatient or on-call consultation service
Access to CT or MRI for structural adrenal and pituitary evaluation
Intensive care capability for patients in refractory shock
keyFacilities: Emergency departments with immediate access to IV hydrocortisone and electrolyte management
Endocrinology inpatient units and outpatient adrenal insufficiency clinics
Pituitary centres for secondary adrenal insufficiency work-up
Intensive care units for refractory hemodynamic instability
preventionStrategies: Carry and know how to use an emergency hydrocortisone injection kit at all times — studies show provision of a home kit reduces crisis-related hospitalization by approximately 50%.
Follow written sick-day rules: double or triple the daily hydrocortisone dose at the first sign of fever, injury, or vomiting; inject 100 mg IM if unable to keep tablets down.
Wear a medical alert bracelet or card specifying adrenal insufficiency and emergency hydrocortisone dose to inform paramedics and emergency staff.
Inform all treating clinicians, dentists, and surgeons about adrenal insufficiency before any procedure, so perioperative glucocorticoid coverage can be planned.
Receive annual influenza vaccination and recommended pneumococcal vaccines to reduce the risk of the febrile illnesses that most commonly precipitate crisis.
Attend annual endocrinology review to reassess replacement doses, update sick-day rule knowledge, and check electrolytes and bone density.
lifestyleModifications: Take hydrocortisone at consistent times daily (typically morning, midday, and early afternoon) to mirror normal cortisol rhythm and minimize fatigue.
Carry extra tablets and an injectable kit when traveling, especially to regions with limited healthcare access.
Avoid activities that deplete circulating volume (prolonged intense heat, extreme endurance exercise) without pre-emptive dose adjustment with the care team.
Limit alcohol, which can mask hypoglycemia and suppress the HPA axis further at high doses.
Keep a symptom diary to recognize the warning signs of inadequate dosing — worsening fatigue, dizziness on standing, salt craving — before they progress.
Educate close family members and colleagues about adrenal crisis recognition and how to administer emergency injection.

recommended: Adequate sodium intake (at least normal dietary sodium) in primary adrenal insufficiency where aldosterone is deficient — low-sodium diets worsen hyponatremia
Small frequent meals to buffer glycemic variability, especially in secondary adrenal insufficiency where hypoglycemia is more prominent
Adequate calcium and vitamin D to offset bone-thinning effects of long-term glucocorticoid replacement
Hydration of at least 2 L per day, increasing with heat or physical activity
avoid: Extreme low-sodium or potassium-restricted diets without specialist supervision in primary adrenal insufficiency
Prolonged fasting without dose adjustment in glucocorticoid-dependent patients
Herbal remedies containing licorice root (glycyrrhizin) which can interact unpredictably with mineralocorticoid balance
Excessive alcohol, which impairs gluconeogenesis and can mask hypoglycemia symptoms

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate aerobic exercise is encouraged and improves cardiovascular conditioning and wellbeing in patients with adrenal insufficiency. Patients should double their hydrocortisone dose or take an extra 5-10 mg tablet approximately 30 minutes before prolonged or intense exercise lasting more than 1 hour, per Endocrine Society guidance. Competitive endurance events require individualized dosing protocols developed with an endocrinologist.
dailyManagement: Take oral hydrocortisone with food at scheduled times; missing doses — especially the morning dose — is the most common trigger of a sub-crisis state.
Check blood pressure and pulse on standing daily if orthostatic symptoms are present, and report consistent drops to the care team.
Monitor blood glucose on days of illness or unusually high exertion; treat values below 4 mmol/L with fast-acting carbohydrates.
Keep emergency injection supplies (hydrocortisone powder, solvent, syringe, needle) unexpired and accessible at home, work, and when traveling.
Wear medical alert identification at all times; update it if medication doses change.
Contact the endocrinology team immediately when unable to tolerate oral medication for more than 2-4 hours due to vomiting or inability to swallow.
prognosis: With timely treatment, adrenal crisis is highly reversible — most patients stabilize within 2-4 hours of IV hydrocortisone and fluid resuscitation and are discharged within 3-7 days. Long-term survival in primary adrenal insufficiency has improved dramatically since the introduction of glucocorticoid replacement in the 1950s; contemporary data show standardized mortality ratios of 1.5-2.0 compared with age-matched controls, driven mainly by cardiovascular disease, infection, and crisis-related deaths. The crisis case-fatality rate in treated, educated patients is approximately 0.5 per 100 patient-years (Smans et al., 2016). Predictors of poor outcome include delayed treatment, unrecognized first-presentation crisis, absence of an emergency kit, and Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome. Patient education — sick-day rules, emergency injection training, medical alert identification — is the single most effective intervention reducing crisis recurrence, with trained patients experiencing roughly half the crisis rate of untrained individuals.
recoveryTimeline: Most patients improve hemodynamically within 2-6 hours of starting IV hydrocortisone and IV fluids. Nausea and vomiting typically resolve within 12-24 hours. Oral hydrocortisone is usually reinstated at 24-48 hours when intake is reliable, with a step-down taper to maintenance dose over 2-3 days. Full return to baseline energy level takes 3-14 days depending on the severity of the precipitating illness.
complications: Persistent hypotension and shock if hydrocortisone is delayed — mortality rises steeply with each hour of untreated crisis.
Severe hypoglycemia causing seizures, loss of consciousness, or permanent neurological injury if glucose replacement is not given alongside hydrocortisone.
Dangerous hyponatremia (sodium below 120 mmol/L) causing cerebral edema, seizures, and respiratory arrest if volume replacement is overly aggressive with hypotonic fluids.
Bilateral adrenal hemorrhage as a cause of crisis that can progress to permanent adrenal insufficiency requiring lifelong replacement even after the acute event resolves.
Osteoporosis from long-term supraphysiological glucocorticoid replacement in patients who require more than standard maintenance dosing — monitor with DXA every 2 years.
Recurrent crises in patients without structured education; each crisis carries risk of cardiovascular complications and death, and recurrent crises predict reduced quality of life.

supportResources.name: Addison's Disease Self Help Group (ADSHG)
supportResources.description: UK patient organization providing sick-day rule guides, emergency injection training resources, and peer support for people with adrenal insufficiency.

supportResources.name: Endocrine Society — Adrenal Insufficiency Guidelines
supportResources.description: Official 2016 clinical practice guideline covering diagnosis, replacement dosing, sick-day rules, and perioperative management.

supportResources.name: National Adrenal Diseases Foundation (NADF)
supportResources.description: US patient advocacy organization with educational resources for patients with Addison disease, pituitary insufficiency, and CAH.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cushing syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both involve the adrenal-pituitary axis, but Cushing syndrome results from cortisol excess (not deficiency), producing obesity, hypertension, and hyperglycemia rather than hypotension and hyponatremia. Diagnosis of Cushing syndrome uses 24-hour urinary free cortisol and dexamethasone suppression tests; addisonian crisis requires urgent cortisol replacement, not suppression.

confusedWithConditions.name: Heart failure
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both can cause hypotension, hyponatremia, and volume disturbance, but heart failure typically features elevated jugular venous pressure, pulmonary crepitations, and elevated BNP. Adrenal crisis presents with volume depletion rather than fluid overload, and responds to glucocorticoid replacement — not diuretics.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypothyroidism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Severe hypothyroidism (myxedema coma) shares fatigue, hyponatremia, and hypotension with adrenal crisis, and both can coexist in autoimmune polyglandular syndrome. Thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) is markedly elevated in primary hypothyroidism; in adrenal crisis, TSH may be mildly elevated but normalizes with cortisol replacement.

relatedConditions.name: Cushing syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: The opposite adrenal disorder — cortisol excess rather than deficiency — managed by the same endocrinology service and sharing adrenal axis physiology.

relatedConditions.name: Hypothyroidism
relatedConditions.relevance: Autoimmune hypothyroidism (Hashimoto disease) co-occurs with autoimmune Addison disease in approximately 20% of cases as part of autoimmune polyglandular syndrome type 2.

relatedConditions.name: Pituitary adenoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Pituitary adenomas causing ACTH deficiency are a leading cause of secondary adrenal insufficiency and adrenal crisis, especially after transsphenoidal surgery.

relatedConditions.name: Autoimmune thyroiditis
relatedConditions.relevance: Hashimoto thyroiditis and Addison disease share autoimmune etiology and co-occur in autoimmune polyglandular syndromes, requiring combined endocrine replacement.

faqs.question: What is an addisonian crisis?
faqs.answer: An addisonian crisis is a life-threatening collapse caused by critically low cortisol and aldosterone levels. It produces severe hypotension, vomiting, and confusion that do not resolve without an injection of hydrocortisone. It is a medical emergency requiring immediate treatment.

faqs.question: What triggers an addisonian crisis?
faqs.answer: The most common triggers are febrile illness, gastroenteritis, surgery, trauma, and excessive physical or emotional stress. Vomiting that prevents absorption of oral glucocorticoid replacement is a particularly dangerous precipitant. In about half of first crises, adrenal insufficiency was not previously diagnosed.

faqs.question: How is an addisonian crisis treated?
faqs.answer: Immediate injection of hydrocortisone 100 mg IV or IM, followed by IV normal saline. A continuous hydrocortisone infusion (200 mg/24 h) is maintained until oral intake resumes. The precipitating cause is treated concurrently. Fludrocortisone is added once oral hydration is possible in primary adrenal insufficiency.

faqs.question: Can an addisonian crisis be fatal?
faqs.answer: Without treatment, adrenal crisis is fatal. With prompt hydrocortisone and fluid resuscitation, recovery is expected within hours. Mortality in treated patients is approximately 0.5 per 100 patient-years; deaths occur mainly from delayed diagnosis or unrecognized first-presentation crisis.

faqs.question: What are the signs of an impending addisonian crisis?
faqs.answer: Warning signs include increasing fatigue, dizziness on standing, nausea, and worsening abdominal pain developing over hours in a person with adrenal insufficiency during illness or stress. Failure to respond to oral dose doubling within 2-4 hours is a sign that injectable hydrocortisone is needed.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Addison disease and addisonian crisis?
faqs.answer: Addison disease is the chronic condition of primary adrenal insufficiency — reduced cortisol and aldosterone production managed with daily replacement tablets. An addisonian crisis is the acute decompensation — a life-threatening emergency in which replacement is insufficient for the body's demands, causing shock and collapse.

faqs.question: How much hydrocortisone is given in an addisonian crisis?
faqs.answer: The Endocrine Society recommends 100 mg hydrocortisone as an IV or IM bolus immediately, followed by 200 mg over the next 24 hours as a continuous infusion or 50 mg every 6 hours. This is 10 times the usual daily maintenance dose and provides both glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid cover.

faqs.question: What blood tests confirm an addisonian crisis?
faqs.answer: Random serum cortisol below 18 µg/dL in a critically ill patient supports the diagnosis; ACTH helps distinguish primary from secondary insufficiency. Electrolytes typically show hyponatremia and hyperkalemia in primary disease. Treatment must not await test results when crisis is clinically suspected.

faqs.question: Can adrenal crisis occur on steroid therapy?
faqs.answer: Yes. Patients on long-term glucocorticoids who stop suddenly, reduce too quickly, or fail to increase their dose during illness develop relative adrenal insufficiency that can progress to crisis. The adrenal glands atrophy during prolonged steroid therapy and cannot respond to physiological stress.

faqs.question: How long does recovery from an addisonian crisis take?
faqs.answer: Blood pressure and nausea typically stabilize within 2-6 hours of IV hydrocortisone. Most patients can take oral hydrocortisone within 24-48 hours and are discharged within 3-7 days. Full return to normal energy levels takes days to 2 weeks depending on the severity of the precipitating illness.

faqs.question: What is the sick day rule for adrenal insufficiency?
faqs.answer: On sick days with fever above 38°C, injury, or vomiting, patients double or triple their daily hydrocortisone dose. If unable to keep tablets down for more than 2 hours due to vomiting, they inject 100 mg hydrocortisone IM and go to an emergency department. All patients should have an emergency kit at home.

faqs.question: Does an addisonian crisis cause high or low blood pressure?
faqs.answer: Addisonian crisis causes critically low blood pressure — systolic often below 90 mmHg — that does not respond to IV fluids alone. Cortisol deficiency removes the permissive effect on vascular tone, and aldosterone deficiency in primary disease causes sodium and volume loss. Hydrocortisone is required to restore vascular responsiveness.

faqs.question: How can an addisonian crisis be prevented?
faqs.answer: Prevention requires sick-day rule education, a home emergency hydrocortisone injection kit, a medical alert bracelet, and annual endocrinology review. Patients who receive formal sick-day rule training experience roughly half the crisis rate of those who do not. Annual influenza vaccination reduces a major precipitant.

faqs.question: Is an addisonian crisis the same as septic shock?
faqs.answer: Both cause hypotension and hemodynamic collapse, but adrenal crisis responds to hydrocortisone rather than requiring vasopressors alone. Infection is the most common trigger of adrenal crisis, so both diagnoses can coexist. In any patient with unexplained shock and hyponatremia, empirical hydrocortisone should be considered.

faqs.question: Can stress alone cause an addisonian crisis?
faqs.answer: Extreme physical stress — major surgery, trauma, or childbirth — can precipitate crisis in a patient with known adrenal insufficiency who has not increased their steroid dose. Emotional stress alone rarely causes a full crisis in adults but may lower the threshold when combined with other precipitants.

symptomKeywords: severe fatigue low blood pressure adrenal
metaDescription: Addisonian crisis: life-threatening adrenal emergency causing hypotension and vomiting. Triggers, diagnosis, hydrocortisone treatment and prevention reviewed.

Rushworth RL, Torpy DJ. 2014. A descriptive study of adrenal crises in adults with adrenal insufficiency: increased risk with age and in those with bacterial infections. BMC Endocr Disord 14:79.
Reisch N, Arlt W. 2009. Fine tuning for quality of life: 21st century approach to treatment of Addison's disease. Endocrinol Metab Clin North Am 38(2):407-18.
Society for Endocrinology. 2020. Adrenal crisis prevention — emergency steroid card and sick day rules. Soc Endocrinol, Bristol.
Allolio B. 2015. Extensive expertise in endocrinology: adrenal crisis. Eur J Endocrinol 172(3):R115-24.
